title: Greek Chicken Souvlaki

ingredients.name: boneless chicken (thighs preferred), cut into 3 cm cubes

subs.name: Boneless mutton pieces work well with a longer marinating time (overnight) and slightly longer cooking; paneer cubes make an excellent vegetarian alternative
Any mild oil; the marinade can also be made with gingelly (sesame) oil for a nutty variation that shifts the dish in a slightly different direction
A mix of dried thyme and basil in equal parts gives a similar Mediterranean herbal quality; fresh pudina (mint) in the marinade is a great Indian adaptation

ingredients.name: thick curd (for marinade, optional but tenderises well)

subs.name: The curd can be omitted entirely; the lemon and olive oil marinade alone produces excellent results

steps.text: In a large bowl, whisk together the olive oil, lemon juice and zest, garlic, oregano, black pepper, and a generous pinch of salt. Add the curd if using — it helps tenderise the chicken and gives a slightly tangier result. Add the chicken cubes and toss well to coat every piece.
Cover the bowl and refrigerate for at least 30 minutes, or up to 4 hours for a deeper flavour. If using wooden skewers, soak them in water for 20 minutes before threading to prevent burning.

Heat a tawa or heavy skillet over high heat until it is very hot — a drop of water should evaporate immediately on contact. Thread the chicken pieces onto skewers (or lay them directly on the tawa if not using skewers). Place the chicken in a single layer without crowding.

Cook the chicken for 5–6 minutes on one side without moving it, so a good char forms. Flip and cook for another 4–5 minutes on the other side. The chicken is done when the juices run clear when pierced with a knife, and the internal temperature (if checked) reaches 75°C. If the pieces are thick, briefly press them flat with a spatula for even cooking.

Remove the souvlaki from the heat and rest for 3 minutes. Serve on warm pita, roti, or rice alongside tzatziki, sliced tomato, and fresh onion rings. A squeeze of lemon over the top just before eating brightens all the flavours.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: chicken → Boneless mutton pieces work well with a longer marinating time (overnight) and slightly longer cooking; paneer cubes make an excellent vegetarian alternative; oregano → A mix of dried thyme and basil in equal parts gives a similar Mediterranean herbal quality; fresh pudina (mint) in the marinade is a great Indian adaptation; curd → The curd can be omitted entirely; the lemon and olive oil marinade alone produces excellent results; olive-oil → Any mild oil; the marinade can also be made with gingelly (sesame) oil for a nutty variation that shifts the dish in a slightly different direction
indian_kitchen_adaptation: A heavy cast-iron tawa or non-stick tawa on high flame produces excellent char and juiciness without a grill. Boneless chicken thighs (available at all meat counters and supermarkets) are the preferred cut for staying juicy. Curd is the ideal tenderiser already found in every Indian kitchen. Dried oregano is available at large grocery chains and spice stores. Serve the finished souvlaki wrapped in roti or naan with homemade tzatziki (made from hung curd) and sliced onion with a squeeze of lemon.
